m_Item1.QuestionText: “Linux is multiuser and multiprocessing system” meaning of this sentence is ____________

Options.OptionText: Multiple users can run seperate jobs.
Single user can run multiple jobs.

m_Item1.Explanation: Multiuser means multiple users can use the system and multi processor means single user can run multiple jobs. So option C.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Any file’s attribute information is stored in which structure on the disk__________________.
m_Item1.Explanation: An inode is a data structure that stores various information about a file in Linux, such as the access mode , ownership, file type, file size, group, number of links, etc. Each inode is identified by an integer number. An inode is assigned to a file when it is created.

m_Item1.QuestionText: For every successful login, which script will be executed?
m_Item1.Explanation: /etc/profile. The /etc/profile script is executed for every successful login in a Unix/Linux system. This script contains system-wide environment variables and startup programs that are executed for every user when they log in.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which among the following interacts directly with system hardware?
m_Item1.Explanation: OS is a collection of multiple programs. Kernel is the only program of  OS  which directly interacts with the system hardware.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Applications communicate with kernel by using:
m_Item1.Explanation: If a system needs kernel , application has to give a system call. A call to kernel is a system call.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Solaris is the name of a flavor of UNIX from :
m_Item1.Explanation: Solaris is a flovour of unix and is owned by Sun Microsystems.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which command is used to display the operating system name:
m_Item1.Explanation: uname will display name of OS.

m_Item1.QuestionText: The directory /opt is reserved for :

Options.OptionText: Installation of add-on application software packages .

m_Item1.QuestionText: Any file or directory present in the ______ directory may not be reserved between the invocation of the program.
m_Item1.Explanation:
/var is a standard subdirectory of the root directory in Linux  that contains files to which the system writes data during the course of its operation.
The /etc directory contains configuration files, which can generally be edited by hand in a text editor.

m_Item1.QuestionText: The directory /opt is reserved for :

Options.OptionText: Installation of add-on application software packages .

m_Item1.Explanation: add-on application software packagesare installed in /opt directory.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which one of the directory does not contain binary files?
m_Item1.Explanation: The /etc directory contains configuration files, which can generally be edited by hand in a text editor. It dose not contain binary files.

m_Item1.QuestionText: The /boot directory stores the data that is used :

Options.OptionText: Before the kernel begins executing user mode programs .
After the kernel begins executing user mode programs .
Before the bootloader is loaded in the RAM .

m_Item1.Explanation: Before the kernel begins executing user mode programs .

m_Item1.QuestionText: Linux filesystem contains mainly :
m_Item1.Explanation: File system contains all above mentioned files

m_Item1.QuestionText: Static files of the bootloader are installed in :
m_Item1.Explanation: /boot directory

m_Item1.QuestionText: The contents of root filesystem is responsible to :
m_Item1.Explanation: The contents of the root filesystem must be adequate to boot, restore, recover, and/or repair the system. To boot a system, enough software and data must be present on the root partition to mount other filesystems.

m_Item1.QuestionText: The Block of every file system contains the major pieces of information about the file system such as file system name ,number of blocks reserved for inodes , free inode list etc
Unix OS was first developed at
Part of the system which manages the resources of computer system, keeps track of the disks, tapes, printers, terminals, communication lines and any other devices.
m_Item1.Explanation: The resource manager is the part of the system that manages the resources of a computer system, including disks, tapes, printers, terminals, communication lines, and any other devices. It keeps track of the status of each device and allocates them to processes as needed.

m_Item1.QuestionText: When we are executing a shell script the shell acts as
m_Item1.Explanation: One of the functionality of OS is to interprete a command. Shell is a program in OS which works as a  command interpreter.

m_Item1.QuestionText: init _______ halts the system
m_Item1.Explanation: The "init" command with the argument "0" halts the system. In Unix/Linux systems, the "init" command is used to control the system's runlevel. The runlevel "0" corresponds to the halt state, which means that the system will be shut down and all processes will be terminated.

m_Item1.QuestionText: A hierarchical structure consisting of directories and files
Which file controls the initialization process?
Names are associated with the IP addresses, so that users do not have to remember IP addresses. This association is the job of the
m_Item1.Explanation: DNS (Domain Name System). DNS is responsible for associating names with IP addresses so that users do not have to remember IP addresses. Instead, users can enter a domain name (such as "google.com") into their web browser, and the DNS system will translate that domain name into the corresponding IP address (such as "172.217.16.46").

m_Item1.QuestionText: New users are added into this file.
m_Item1.Explanation: In Unix/Linux systems, new users are added into the /etc/passwd file. This file contains information about each user account on the system, including the username, user ID, group ID, home directory, and login shell.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Who is credited with the creation of the Linux kernel in 1991?
m_Item1.Explanation: Linus Torvalds, a Finnish software engineer, created the Linux kernel in 1991 as a hobby project during his studies at the University of Helsinki.

m_Item1.QuestionText: The Linux operating system was inspired by which other operating system?
m_Item1.Explanation: Linux was inspired by MINIX, a Unix-like operating system designed for educational purposes by Andrew S. Tanenbaum.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which license governs the distribution of the Linux kernel?
m_Item1.Explanation: The Linux kernel is distributed under the GNU General Public License (GPL), which allows users to freely use, modify, and distribute the software.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the mascot of Linux and what type of animal is it?
m_Item1.Explanation: Tux, the Penguin, is the official mascot of Linux, chosen for its playful and friendly nature.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which major company announced support for Linux in 1999, boosting its credibility in the business community?
m_Item1.Explanation: In 1999, IBM announced full support for Linux, investing heavily in Linux-based projects and services, thereby enhancing its credibility in the business community.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What was the first graphical user interface for Linux called?
m_Item1.Explanation: XFree86 was one of the first widely used graphical user interfaces for Linux, providing a foundation for further development in Linux desktop environments.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which major Linux distribution was created by Ian Murdock in 1993?
m_Item1.Explanation: Debian, one of the most influential Linux distributions, was founded by Ian Murdock in 1993 and has been the basis for many other distributions.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which component of the Linux architecture directly interacts with the hardware?
m_Item1.Explanation: The kernel is the core component of the Linux operating system that directly interacts with hardware and manages system resources.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the role of the shell in the Linux architecture?

Options.OptionText: To provide an interface between the user and the kernel
To prevent user access to the system

m_Item1.Explanation: The shell provides a command-line interface that allows users to interact with the kernel and execute commands.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is NOT a type of shell in Linux?
m_Item1.Explanation: Wsh is not a recognized shell in Linux. Bash, Zsh, and Csh are types of shells available in Linux.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which Linux component handles tasks such as device management and memory management?
m_Item1.Explanation: The kernel handles core system functions such as device management, memory management, and process management.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which architecture component allows users to execute custom commands and scripts?
m_Item1.Explanation: The shell allows users to execute custom commands and scripts, serving as a command interpreter between the user and the system.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which part of the Linux architecture handles external command execution?
m_Item1.Explanation: Command interpreters (or shells) are responsible for reading and executing commands input by the users.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Where are the essential commands and utilities for Linux housed?
m_Item1.Explanation: The /bin directory contains essential user command binaries (programs), which are necessary for minimal system operations.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which command is used to update the package repository cache in Ubuntu?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'apt-get update' command is used to download the package lists from the repositories and update the package information.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the default package manager for CentOS?
m_Item1.Explanation: Yum (Yellowdog Updater, Modified) is the default package manager used in CentOS to manage the software packages.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In Ubuntu, which file is modified to configure a static IP address?
m_Item1.Explanation: The '/etc/network/interfaces' file is used for network interface configuration, including assigning a static IP address in Ubuntu.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the command to start the Apache service in CentOS 8?
m_Item1.Explanation: In CentOS, the 'httpd' service is used for Apache. 'systemctl start httpd' will start the Apache service.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which command is used to display disk usage statistics of a specified directory in Linux?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'du' command is used in Linux to display disk usage statistics for files and directories.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the purpose of the 'fstab' file in Linux systems?

Options.OptionText: To define file systems to be mounted at boot

m_Item1.Explanation: The '/etc/fstab' file contains information about file systems and specifies how they should be automatically mounted.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In Ubuntu, which command will set the execute permission only for the user on a file named 'script.sh'?
m_Item1.Explanation: 'chmod u+x script.sh' adds execute permission for the user (owner) of the file 'script.sh'.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which command should be used to find the path of an installed package in CentOS?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'rpm -ql' command lists all files that belong to the installed RPM package, showing the path for each.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which command is used to log out of a terminal session in Linux?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'exit' command is commonly used to terminate a terminal session and log out in Linux.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the purpose of the 'su' command in Linux?

Options.OptionText: To switch to another user temporarily
To log out of the system

m_Item1.Explanation: The 'su' command is used to switch the current user to another user without logging out.

m_Item1.QuestionText: When logging in to a Linux system, which file typically determines the shell that a user will enter?
m_Item1.Explanation: The '/etc/passwd' file contains information about user accounts, including the default shell them.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following signals is sent by default when using 'logout' to close a shell session?
m_Item1.Explanation: 'logout' sends a hang-up (SIGHUP) signal to terminate the terminal session.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which file can be edited to change the welcome message shown to users upon logging in via SSH?
m_Item1.Explanation: The '/etc/motd' file, which stands for 'Message of the Day,' is used to display a welcome message after a successful login.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which command can be used to initiate a new login session in a terminal window without logging out of the current session?
m_Item1.Explanation: Using the 'login' command starts a new login session in a terminal.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What does the ‘who’ command display in a Linux system?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'who' command gives a list of all users currently logged into the system.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In Linux, what is the use of the '.bash_logout' file in a user's home directory?
m_Item1.Explanation: The '.bash_logout' file contains commands that are executed when logging out of a bash shell session.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which command is used to display the disk usage of a folder in the Linux file system?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'du' command is used to estimate file space usage, which displays the disk usage of a folder or file.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary purpose of the 'chmod' command in Linux?

Options.OptionText: To change the owner of a file

m_Item1.Explanation: The 'chmod' command is used to change the permission settings of a file or directory.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which file system is considered the default file system for most Linux distributions?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'ext4' file system is often the default file system for most Linux distributions due to its robustness and improved performance.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the role of an inode in the Linux file system?

Options.OptionText: To store metadata about a file

m_Item1.Explanation: An inode is a data structure that stores metadata about a file, such as ownership, permissions, and file type.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which command in Linux can be used to create a new empty file or update the timestamp of an existing file?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'touch' command is used to create a new empty file or update the modification and access timestamps of an existing file.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary purpose of the mount command in Linux?

Options.OptionText: To attach a filesystem to a directory tree

m_Item1.Explanation: The mount command attaches a filesystem to a specified directory within the directory tree, allowing access to its contents.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following commands would you use to safely detach a filesystem from the directory tree?
m_Item1.Explanation: The umount command is used to safely detach a filesystem from the directory tree in Linux.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Where are the current filesystem mount points listed in Linux?
m_Item1.Explanation: /proc/mounts contains information about all the current mount points, reflecting the status of mounted filesystems.

m_Item1.QuestionText: If a device is being used and cannot be unmounted, which of the following might prevent the umount command from succeeding?

Options.OptionText: The mount point is set to read-only
The device does not have a filesystem
A user has a file open on the filesystem
The system is in single-user mode

m_Item1.Explanation: If a device is in use, with files open or a user's working directory situated on it, the umount command will fail until those files are closed.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In Linux, what file typically contains the configuration for auto-mounting filesystems on boot?
m_Item1.Explanation: /etc/fstab is the configuration file that specifies how drives should be automatically mounted during boot in Linux.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is operating system?

Options.OptionText: collection of programs that manages hardware resources
system service provider to the application programs
link to interface the hardware and application programs

m_Item1.Explanation: An operating system (OS) is a collection of programs that manages hardware resources, provides system services to application programs, and acts as a link to interface the hardware and application programs. It is responsible for managing the computer's memory, processes, files, and input/output devices. The OS provides a layer of abstraction between the hardware and software, allowing applications to run on different hardware platforms without modification. The OS also provides a user interface, allowing users to interact with the computer and its resources.

m_Item1.QuestionText: To access the services of operating system, the interface is provided by the
m_Item1.Explanation: System calls are the interface provided by the operating system to access its services. They allow user programs to request services from the kernel, such as I/O operations, process management, and memory management. System calls are typically implemented as functions in a library, and the library provides a higher-level API for accessing the system calls. The API provides a more convenient and portable interface for application developers to use. Assembly instructions are low-level instructions used to program the CPU directly, and they are not used to access operating system services.

m_Item1.QuestionText: ________________ is an interface to the kernel which hides the complexity of the kernel function from the user.  it takes commands from the user and executes the kernel functions.
m_Item1.Explanation: A shell is an interface to the kernel which hides the complexity of kernel functions from the user. It takes commands from the user and executes the kernel functions. The shell provides a command-line interface that allows users to interact with the operating system by entering commands. The shell interprets the commands and executes the corresponding kernel functions.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Find /   -name ‘p*’ will

Options.OptionText: List all files starting with p in / i.e root and it's subdirectories.
List a file by name p* in / i.e root directory
List all the files starting with p only in / i.e root directory

m_Item1.Explanation:
Find / means searching any file in root directory and it's sub directories i.e /
-name 'p*' means all files starting with p.
hence command will find all files starting with p in root directorie and its subdirectories.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Among the following command which is used with vi editors to delete a single character?
m_Item1.Explanation: In the vi editor, the "x" command is used to delete a single character. The "x" command deletes the character under the cursor and moves the cursor to the right.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is used to represent the top-level directory?
m_Item1.Explanation: / is a root directory and is at the top level.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What command is used to display characteristics of process?
m_Item1.Explanation: $ps command displays process status of the commands.

m_Item1.QuestionText: _________ represents user's home directory
m_Item1.Explanation: Write explaination here..
